name: Binaryen
summary: WebAssembly-oriented compiler toolkit and optimizer with its own structured IR designed around Wasm's validation and structured-control-flow constraints. Used by Emscripten, AssemblyScript, and as a post-processing optimizer for many wasm-emitting toolchains.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Binaryen is a compiler toolkit centered on WebAssembly, with its own IR and optimization pipeline for wasm generation, transformation, and simplification. It is a useful concrete example of a backend and IR ecosystem tuned to a specific portable target.

Production adopters: Emscripten uses Binaryen for post-LLVM wasm optimization; AssemblyScript uses it directly as the compiler backend; wasm-bindgen's `wasm-opt` is Binaryen with a different CLI; many wasm-emitting toolchains run Binaryen as a final optimization pass on emitted wasm.

sections.title: Structured wasm IR
sections.content:
Binaryen's IR is shaped by WebAssembly's structured control-flow model rather than treating wasm as another machine backend. Wasm has `block`/`loop`/`if`/`else` rather than arbitrary jumps, so Binaryen's IR is also structured — a tree of blocks rather than a CFG of basic blocks. This is the dual of LLVM/Cranelift, where SSA over basic blocks is the canonical form.

The choice trades some optimization-algorithm familiarity (most SSA optimization literature assumes a CFG) for natural fit with the validation and codegen target.

sections.title: Optimization shape
sections.content:
Binaryen optimizations are shaped by wasm's structured, validated execution model and by the practical needs of compilers that emit portable sandboxed code: code-size minimization (download size matters for browser delivery), control-flow simplification (collapsing redundant blocks), inlining bounded by code-size budgets, and wasm-specific constructs like dropping side-effect-free `local.tee` results.

This is why Binaryen runs as a *post-pass* on wasm produced by other toolchains: LLVM produces correct wasm, but Binaryen knows wasm-specific rewrites that LLVM does not — primarily because LLVM's optimizer assumes a CFG-of-basic-blocks model that wasm cannot directly express.

sections.title: Where Binaryen fits
sections.content:
Binaryen is best understood as a specialized backend and transformation toolkit for WebAssembly-oriented workflows. It is most valuable where wasm is not just an output format but a real architectural target with its own representation and optimization logic.

For a language designer targeting wasm: emit through LLVM if rich C-family optimization is wanted at the cost of some wasm-specific rewrites; emit through Binaryen directly (AssemblyScript path) if wasm-shape control matters more than reusing LLVM's optimizer; or emit through LLVM and post-process with Binaryen to get both.
